summary: Baidu’s ERNIE-Image emerged as the strongest concrete artifact in the set: an open 8B-parameter text-to-image model built on a single-stream Diffusion Transformer that claims state-of-the-art performance among open-weight image generators. ByteDance Seed also introduced Seed Full-Duplex Speech LLM for real-time conversational audio, while Epoch AI’s MirrorCode added evidence that frontier models can complete some weeks-long software tasks, a meaningful signal for coding-agent roadmaps.

KEY_TAKEAWAYS: ByteDance Seed described Seed Full-Duplex Speech LLM as focused on attentive listening, robust interference suppression, and more natural real-time interaction.
The launch sits alongside ByteDance Seed’s Dola-Seed-2.0-Preview release on Arena, suggesting an active model rollout cadence across both speech and general model lines.
The emphasis on full-duplex interaction matters because it targets simultaneous listening-and-speaking behavior, a capability gap in many current turn-based voice assistants.

top_topics.title: baidu/ERNIE-Image
top_topics.summary: Baidu's ERNIE-Image repository trended on GitHub as an open text-to-image generation model. The project describes a single-stream Diffusion Transformer architecture with 8B DiT parameters.

items.title: baidu/ERNIE-Image
items.summary: ERNIE-Image is an open text-to-image generation model developed by the ERNIE-Image team at Baidu. It is built on a single-stream Diffusion Transformer (DiT), with only 8B DiT parameters, it reaches state-of-the-art performance among open-weight text-to-image models.

top_topics.SHORT_COMMENT: Baidu's ERNIE-Image repository trended on GitHub as an open text-to-image generation model. The project describes a single-stream Diffusion Transformer architecture with 8B DiT parameters.
top_topics.WHY_IT_MATTERS: An 8B open-weight image model with a state-of-the-art claim could shift enterprise and startup build-vs-buy decisions in generative media.
top_topics.HEADLINE: Baidu Releases ERNIE-Image, an 8B Open-Weight Text-to-Image Model
KEY_TAKEAWAYS: Baidu published ERNIE-Image as an open text-to-image model rather than a closed API-only system, increasing its relevance for self-hosted and fine-tuned deployments.
The model is built on a single-stream Diffusion Transformer architecture and uses 8B DiT parameters, a concrete scale point for teams benchmarking open image stacks.

The repository description claims state-of-the-art performance among open-weight text-to-image models, positioning ERNIE-Image directly against other open image generators rather than proprietary systems.

items.title: AIs can now often do massive easy-to-verify SWE tasks and I’ve updated towards shorter timelines (LessWrong)
MirrorCode: Evidence that AI can already do some weeks-long coding tasks (Epoch AI)

top_topics.SHORT_COMMENT: Import AI highlighted Epoch AI's MirrorCode work as evidence that AI can already complete some coding tasks spanning multiple weeks. The item frames MirrorCode as empirical support for longer-horizon software task capability.
top_topics.WHY_IT_MATTERS: If replicated, this would materially affect expectations for software automation timelines, agent product scope, and infrastructure demand for long-horizon coding workflows.
top_topics.HEADLINE: Epoch AI’s MirrorCode Reports AI Can Complete Some Weeks-Long Coding Tasks
KEY_TAKEAWAYS: MirrorCode is framed as evidence that AI systems can already handle some software engineering tasks that would take humans weeks, extending the horizon beyond short benchmark exercises.
The topic was amplified through Import AI, which linked the result to updated expectations around AI performance on large, easy-to-verify software engineering tasks.
For executives, the practical implication is not generic coding assistance but potential expansion into longer-duration agent workflows where outputs can be automatically tested or verified.

topics.title: Changes in the system prompt between Claude Opus 4.6 and 4.7
topics.summary: Simon Willison analyzed the differences between the published system prompts for Claude Opus 4.6 and Claude Opus 4.7. The post highlights prompt-level changes in Anthropic's user-facing chat system instructions.

items.title: Changes in the system prompt between Claude Opus 4.6 and 4.7
items.summary:
Anthropic are the only major AI lab to publish the system prompts for their user-facing chat systems. Their system prompt archive now dates all the way back to Claude 3 in July 2024 and it's always interesting to see how the system prompt evolves as they publish new models.

Opus 4.7 shipped the other day (April 16, 2026) with a Claude.ai system prompt update since Opus 4.6 (February 5, 2026).

I had Claude Code take the Markdown version of their system prompts, break that up into separate documents for each of the models and then construct

topics.SHORT_COMMENT: Simon Willison analyzed the differences between the published system prompts for Claude Opus 4.6 and Claude Opus 4.7. The post highlights prompt-level changes in Anthropic's user-facing chat system instructions.
topics.WHY_IT_MATTERS: This research paper surfaced across 1 independent source(s).
topics.HEADLINE: Changes in the system prompt between Claude Opus 4.6 and 4.7

topics.title: Claude system prompts as a git timeline
topics.summary: Simon Willison published an analysis that reconstructs Claude system prompts as a git-style timeline. The post uses Anthropic's published system prompts to examine how the prompts changed over time.

items.title: Claude system prompts as a git timeline
items.summary:
Research: Claude system prompts as a git timeline

Anthropic publish the system prompts for Claude chat and make that page available as Markdown. I had Claude Code turn that page into separate files for each model and model family with fake git commit dates to enable browsing the changes via the GitHub commit view.

I used this to write my own detailed notes on the changes between Opus 4.6 and 4.7.

Tags:

topics.SHORT_COMMENT: Simon Willison published an analysis that reconstructs Claude system prompts as a git-style timeline. The post uses Anthropic's published system prompts to examine how the prompts changed over time.
topics.WHY_IT_MATTERS: This research paper surfaced across 1 independent source(s).
topics.HEADLINE: Claude system prompts as a git timeline

topics.title: Join us at PyCon US 2026 in Long Beach - we have new AI and security tracks this year
topics.summary: Simon Willison announced that PyCon US 2026 in Long Beach will include new AI and security tracks. The post provides scheduling and conference information for the upcoming event.

items.title: Join us at PyCon US 2026 in Long Beach - we have new AI and security tracks this year
items.summary:
This year's PyCon US is coming up next month from May 13th to May 19th, with the core conference talks from Friday 15th to Sunday 17th and tutorial and sprint days either side. It's in Long Beach, California this year, the first time PyCon US has come to the West Coast since Portland, Oregon in 2017 and the first time in California since Santa Clara in 2013.

If you're based in California this is a great opportunity to catch up with the Python community, meet a whole lot of interesting people and learn a ton of interesting things.

In addition to regular PyCon programming we have two new dedicated tracks at the conference this year: an AI track on Friday and a

topics.title: Adding a new content type to my blog-to-newsletter tool
topics.summary: Simon Willison wrote about adding a new content type to his blog-to-newsletter tool. The post uses a prompt example connected to his Agentic Engineering Patterns material to explain the implementation.

items.title: Adding a new content type to my blog-to-newsletter tool
items.summary:
Agentic Engineering Patterns >

Here's an example of a deceptively short prompt that got a quite a lot of work done in a single shot.

First, some background. I send out a free Substack newsletter around once a week containing content copied-and-pasted from my blog. I'm effectively using Substack as a lightweight way to allow people to subscribe to my blog via email.

I generate the newsletter with my blog-to-newsletter tool - an HTML and JavaScript app that fetches my latest content from this Datasette instance and format...
